claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children would you like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for your help. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, eligibility depends on whether he's still in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in full-time education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, or similar qualifications)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's in sixth form college studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Studying A-levels at a sixth form college counts as approved education, which is good for eligibility.

Now I have a few more questions about your circumstances:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know.

**Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his upkeep (for example, on food, clothes, or pocket money)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to check:

**Does Alex work 24 hours or more per week, or receive any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work that many hours and he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for that information.

One final question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**. 🎉

Here's a summary of why:

- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ Alex lives with you, meaning you're responsible for him
- ✅ Although Alex is 17 (over 16), he's in approved education — studying A-levels at sixth form college qualifies
- ✅ Alex doesn't work 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Alex doesn't receive benefits in his own right
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

You can continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex as long as he remains in approved education or training, up until he turns 20.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit through the official government channels. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which is over 16 but under 20. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue if the child stays in approved education or training. Alex is studying A-levels at a sixth form college, which qualifies as approved education. The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives with them, meaning the claimant is responsible for the child. Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in his own right (which would stop eligibility). No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. They live in the UK, Alex lives with them, and although Alex is 17, he remains in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Alex does not work excessive hours or claim benefits himself, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

Alex is studying A-levels at a sixth form college, which qualifies as approved education. The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives with them, meaning the claimant is responsible for the child. Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in his own right (which would stop eligibility). No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. They live in the UK, Alex lives with them, and although Alex is 17, he remains in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Alex does not work excessive hours or claim benefits himself, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.
